text: Hydraulic head characteristics were within +1 to -3 ft of manufacturers data for a given constant speed.
The best configuration removed carbon dioxide from water at approximately 99% of the ideal removal rate when the stripping flow was 65 gpm and the sweep gas flow rate was 4 scfm.

The Molten Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) is to be a low-pressure, high-temperature, graphite moderated circulating fuel nuclear reactor using fissile and fertile materials dissolved in molten fluoride salts and is designed for a heat generation rate of 10 Mw (1, 2, and 3).

Much of the design of the fuel pump was derived from the past experience with similar pumps for elevated temperature service which were developed during the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Program at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (4, 5, and 6).

1 and includes a centrifugal impeller and volute with the impeller supported at the lower end of a vertical shaft, grease-lubricated bearings for supporting the shaft, bearing housing, pump tank bowl, and volute support.
The pump was driven with a 60 hp d.c. variable speed motor.
A bundle of 1-in. diameter thin-wall tubes, 6-in. long, was added to the lower end of this pipe to reduce rotation of the water column.
Configuration 2 is shown in Fig.
One beaker contained 84 spray holes, 1/8-in. in diameter, and the other contained 30 spray holes, 1/4-in. in diameter.
Configuration 4 was the same as No.
3, except the number of holes was reduced by a factor of two and the spray was directed radially inwards toward the pump shaft.
Configuration 5 was a toroid constructed of pipe as shown in Fig.
6, and located in the pump tank as shown in Fig.
Each stripper contained two rows of 80 holes each, $\frac{1}{16}$ -in. in diameter.
Individual pressures at the inlet and throat were indicated on Bourdon tube gages, 0-30 psi range, 1/8 psi subdivision, and $1 / 4\%$ accuracy.
The differential pressure transmitter was read out on a differential gage, 0-50 psi range, 1/2 psi subdivision, $1 / 4\%$ accuracy.
The flow is estimated to be accurate within $\pm 3\%$ .
The discharge pressure was measured on a Bourdon tube gage, 0-100 psi range, $\frac{1}{2}$ psi subdivision, and $\frac{1}{4\%}$ accuracy.
The pump shaft speed was measured by use of a 60-tooth gear mounted on the shaft, a magnetic pickup, and a counter which indicated directly in rpm.
The water temperature was measured with a dial-type thermometer, 0 to $240\mathrm{F}$ range, 2 F subdivision.
Motor input power data was obtained by two methods: power recorder, 0 to 40 kw range, 0.8 kw subdivision and power analyzer which indicated
The fountain flow was measured by directing the flow through $90^{\circ}$ V-notch weirs and measuring the height of the flow column.
The pH value of the water was indicated with a Beckman pH meter, Model H-2, range 0 to $14\mathrm{pH}$ with an accuracy of 0.03 pH.
The pump tank liquid level was indicated with a scale marked off in 0.l-in. divisions.
Hydraulic performance data were obtained over a wide range of operating conditions with impellers of 11- and 13-in. outside diameter.

Two methods of operation were used: speed was varied (700 to 1300 rpm) at constant system resistance for several values of resistance with the 13-in. impeller, and system resistance was varied at constant speed for several values of speed (700 to 1300 rpm) with the 11-in. impeller.

A number of tests were performed with both impeller diameters to ascertain the change in effectiveness of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ removal caused by various stripper configurations, flow rates, jet velocities, and sweep gas flow rates.
Readings of pH of the water were taken versus time to determine the time required to reduce the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ concentration by a factor of two.
A total of 37 tests were performed.
Hydraulic performance data were obtained over a wide range of head and flow conditions at several speeds for the 8 in. x 6 in. volute, using impellers of l3- and ll-in. outside diameter.
9, which is a plot of head versus flow at various speeds.
10, which is a plot of head versus flow at various speeds.
11; it had the effect of increasing the head at the lower range of flows on a constant speed line.
13, superimposed on a plot of head-flow-speed data.
In the stripping tests, data were obtained to determine the time required to reduce the $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ concentration by one half (half-life).
The change in pH value of the distilled water was measured over a range from 4 to 6 versus time.
For plotting purposes the pH values were converted to the logarithm of the molarity of $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ to determine the half-life.
Theoretical half-life $(t = 0.69\mathrm{V} / \mathbf{Q}_{\mathrm{s}})$ was computed for each test and compared with the experimental half-life to give relative effectiveness.
Values of relative effectiveness ranged from 10 to 40 percent.
In tests 17 through $2^{\frac{1}{4}}$ , the flow and jet velocity were varied simultaneously at a constant sweep gas flow rate.
The relative effectiveness varied from 27 to 99 percent.
17) revealed the need to control it; the slinger impeller was causing an undesirable spray.

Configuration 4 was used with the ll-in. diameter impeller and the fountain flow was measured at various speeds along a constant resistance line defined by $1300\mathrm{gpm}$ and 45 ft.
Configuration 4 was adopted for use on the prototype MSRE fuel pump.
A test was performed with the 13-in. diameter impeller operating at $1450\mathrm{~gpm}$ , 50 ft head, and starting liquid level at $1\frac{1}{2}$ -in. above center line of the volute.
In-gassing began at approximately $4\frac{1}{2}$ -in. below the center line of the volute.
At $5\frac{1}{2}$ in. below the center line of the volute, the system flow dropped to $700\mathrm{~gpm}$ , and $6\frac{1}{2}$ in. below the center line of the volute, the flow reduced to zero.
A test was performed with the ll-in. impeller operating at 1250 gpm, 45 ft and starting liquid level at 1 l/2 in. above the center line of the volute.
Ingassing began at approximately 3 in. below the center line of the volute.
Vigorous ingassing and loss of head and flow began at 3 l/2 in. below the volute center line.
From the experimental hydraulic characteristics, the total head was found to deviate from reported data by $+1$ to $-3$ ft.
Based on the water test results, a 11.5-in. diameter impeller will be required to meet the reactor design head and flow (48.5 ft and 1200 gpm).
Gas stripping was accomplished in the pump tank with a relative effectiveness of up to 99 percent.
